comment.body:
Question for you Alma, what about methods that are kind of in between traditional technicals and things like higher levels quant math? I’ve studied JM Hurst’s work a ton and it’s centered around frequency and amplitude analysis (spectral and phasing analysis with filters). It’s all about timing instead of price analysis through signal processing  methods like Fourier analysis (although much more sophisticated methods are used now). “Modern” Study linked here (2014): 

https://ucf.digital.flvc.org/islandora/object/ucf:45283

Also, when are the many signals/indicators to read? Do you just feed in all of these signals into your model for probabilistic determinations like these firms do? Is there ever a point of having too much data like overfitting? If so how do filter out the unnecessary data/signals/indicators?

comment.body:
For the 2nd Q... forget 'Wyckoff method'. There is no 'Wyckoff method', thats only a made up thing by furus who sold methods to get money. 
Read only what Wyckoff said himself, and not articles. He literally explains how the market works. 
Thats it. 

When something becomes 'methodoligized' thats always the lack of understanding of the essence

comment.body:
Thanks for the super helpful posts Alma. I have couple of questions.

1. Are not most indicators lagging. For e.g. RSI only moves after price moves and not before. Options flow is the only one I know to be leading. Are there any other leading indicators ?

2. What is your opinion about smart money concepts which derived from Wyckoff. Equal lows indicating buy liquidity zones, equal highs sell zones. Fair value gaps etc. Market retracing to FVG which matches with your analogy of offering fair value ?

2. What is your opinion about smart money concepts which derived from Wyckoff. Equal lows indicating buy liquidity zones, equal highs sell zones. Fair value gaps etc. Market retracing to FVG which matches with your analogy of offering fair value ?

comment.body:
This overfitting question... 
Yeah, there is no exact answer for that. Be careful, bcs scientists as well, try to formalize everything, hoping there is still free lunch somewhere . This is a wrong approach and the purpose of science is different. 
It is all acreative process.
Anyways... 

You have to know whats the purpose, and how the formulas, metrics work, what they do to know what you can expect from them and what not. 

For me, it is making rough probability distributions from historical datas on vol and the using this, i meassure the momentum of the price action, and use the vol paths, to make a price distribution. 
Keep it simple. 

By meassuring the momentum, it is all about to check whether a momentum is accelerating or deccelerating. 
This is what you try to project from the past datas, bcs if the will is there, there must be signs. And thats the purpose of all of those models. To reveal these tendencies.
